records.title: "We've Got the Power": Sandia technology test delivers electricity to the grid
records.authors_or_org: Sandia National Laboratories

records.notes: Directly fetched; primary national-lab source. | Directly fetched by scout; primary national-lab source. Verifier confirmed no reason to doubt; retained as-is.

records.title: 초임계 이산화탄소 발전 시스템, 국내 최초 '분기점' 달성 (Supercritical CO2 Power Generation System Achieves Korea's First 'Break-Even Point')
records.authors_or_org: Korea Atomic Energy Research Institute (KAERI)
records.claim_supported: KAERI, with consortium partners Jinsol Turbo Machinery, KAIST, and POSTECH, achieved a 100 kW sCO2 power-generation 'break-even point' (output power exceeding input power) in April 2024, reported 2024-10-23 -- Korea's first such demonstration, compact to 1/10-1/20 the size of comparable steam systems.
records.notes: Directly fetched via Korean-language query; primary Korean national-lab demonstration record with named industry/academic consortium partners. | Directly fetched by scout via Korean-language query; primary Korean national-lab demonstration record with named industry/academic consortium partners. Retained as-is.

records.title: 工程热物理所在超临界 CO2 回热器热惯性研究方面取得进展 (Progress on Thermal Inertia of Supercritical CO2 Recuperator at the Institute of Engineering Thermophysics)
records.authors_or_org: Institute of Engineering Thermophysics, Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS-IET)
records.claim_supported: CAS Institute of Engineering Thermophysics, with Xi'an Jiaotong University and Tsinghua University, engineered a hundred-kilowatt-class rapid-response PCHE recuperator for sCO2 Brayton cycles: up to 22C/min permissible heating rate, 129.4 kW peak duty, >95% heat-exchange efficiency, stabilizing within 30-60 seconds after sudden flow changes; work funded under CAS Youth Team Program and National Key R&D Program (No. 2022YFB4100100), published 2025-01-26.
records.notes: Directly fetched via Chinese-language query; primary Chinese national-lab R&D record with named university partners and grant number. | Directly fetched by scout via Chinese-language query; primary Chinese national-lab R&D record with named university partners and grant number. Retained as-is.

records.title: Performance Test Codes (PTC 6, PTC 6.2, PTC 22, PTC 6S)
records.authors_or_org: American Society of Mechanical Engineers (ASME)
records.claim_supported: ASME publishes 48 Performance Test Codes covering power production, combustion/heat transfer, fluid handling, and emissions, including PTC 6 (steam turbines), PTC 6.2 (steam turbines in combined cycles), and PTC 22 (gas turbines); no sCO2-specific PTC was identified in this session, indicating a possible standards gap for sCO2 turbomachinery acceptance testing.
records.notes: Directly fetched. The apparent absence of an sCO2-specific PTC is a notable gap worth flagging in the lane brief bottlenecks section, though absence-of-evidence should be treated cautiously (ASME may have newer/draft codes not indexed on this page). | Directly fetched by scout. The apparent absence of an sCO2-specific PTC is a notable gap worth flagging in the lane brief bottlenecks section, though absence-of-evidence should be treated cautiously (ASME may have newer/draft codes not indexed on this page).

records.title: Part 53 -- Risk-Informed, Technology-Inclusive Regulatory Framework for Commercial Nuclear Plants
records.authors_or_org: US Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC)
records.claim_supported: NRC Commissioners voted 2026-03-26 to finalize 10 CFR Part 53, a new risk-informed, performance-based, technology-inclusive licensing framework for advanced reactors, in response to the Nuclear Energy Innovation and Modernization Act (NEIMA); published in the Federal Register 2026-04-03, effective ~2026-05-03 (30 days after publication) -- a regulatory trigger relevant to licensing non-LWR balance-of-plant/power-conversion systems (sCO2, molten-salt, sodium-cooled).
records.notes: Not independently fetched this session; effective date and rule number should be reconfirmed by the verifier via a successful direct fetch. | Effective date corrected from the scout's unverified '2026-04-29' to '~2026-05-03' (30 days after the 2026-04-03 Federal Register publication) per the ANS Nuclear Newswire primary-adjacent confirmation. Recommend a follow-up direct fetch of federalregister.gov or nrc.gov once the unblock/timeout issue clears, to pin the exact effective date precisely.

records.title: Enhanced geothermal systems could expand geothermal power generation
records.authors_or_org: US Energy Information Administration (EIA)
records.claim_supported: EIA "Today in Energy" analysis (published 2026-02-19) on how enhanced geothermal systems (EGS), using oil-and-gas horizontal-drilling and fracking techniques, could expand US geothermal power generation beyond the current 2.7 GW (0.2% of total) of conventional hydrothermal capacity; cites USGS estimate of 135 GW EGS potential in the Great Basin alone and up to 150 GW of cost-effective EGS generation achievable in coming decades; notes Fervo's Cape Station (Utah), the first large-scale commercial EGS generator in the US, with 28 MW net summer capacity scheduled for June 2026.
records.notes: Not fetched directly; verifier should confirm figures via direct fetch. | Directly fetched and confirmed this session (scout had only used WebSearch synthesis).

records.title: Renewables for Industry -- Executive Summary
records.authors_or_org: International Energy Agency (IEA)
records.claim_supported: Low-temperature-heat sectors are ~70% of global industrial energy consumption and generated ~3 Gt CO2 in 2023 (half of direct industrial emissions, an 8% decline since 2013); thermal storage costs ~$15-20/kWh vs. chemical batteries; EU heat-electrification technical potential is ~600 TWh/yr additional electricity demand, China's is ~1,700 TWh/yr (comparable to projected solar PV growth to 2030); industrial heat-pump operating costs in the EU span EUR41-74/MWh.
records.notes: Directly fetched; treated as source_type government since IEA is an intergovernmental agency analogous to a national statistical/policy body -- flagged for verifier to confirm classification fit. Figures are load-bearing market/technology-cost data used for triangulation against the China-specific market estimate in L04-051. | Kept as source_type government per scout's original classification (IEA is an intergovernmental agency producing a primary official report, closer to the T1 'government/national-lab primary record' bucket than to 'market_industry' consultancy-style estimates). This means the lane still has a gap against the '>=3 market/industry' mix target -- see verifier's return summary. Figures are load-bearing market/technology-cost data used for triangulation against the China-specific market estimate in L04-051; not independently triangulated against a second body this session.

records.title: Energy Efficiency Grant Call for Power Generation Companies
records.authors_or_org: Energy Market Authority of Singapore (EMA)
records.claim_supported: Singapore's EMA Energy Efficiency Grant Call for power-generation companies (Gencos) awarded a total of S$23 million to three named generation companies -- Senoko Energy, Tuas Power Generation, and YTL PowerSeraya -- for energy-efficiency retrofit projects (capped at 50% of qualifying cost), expected to achieve over 30 kilotons/yr of carbon abatement; application for this call has since closed.
records.notes: English is an official administrative language in Singapore, so this does not count toward the zh/ja/ko local-language quota, though it is a genuine Singapore-geography primary government source. | Demand_evidence_type upgraded from scout's 'buyer_specification' to 'official_project_award' since the fetched page names specific awardee companies and a specific dollar figure, i.e. a concluded award rather than an open specification. English is an official administrative language in Singapore, so this does not count toward the zh/ja/ko local-language quota, though it is a genuine Singapore-geography primary government source.

records.title: Supercritical CO2 Brayton Power Cycle Pilot Plant Bolstered with $80M in Federal Funding
records.authors_or_org: POWER Magazine
records.claim_supported: DOE/NETL awarded up to $80 million (announced 2016-10-18) to a 10 MWe sCO2 Brayton-cycle pilot plant at SwRI's San Antonio campus, led by GTI with GE Global Research, targeting >50% cycle efficiency at a minimum 700C turbine inlet temperature -- advancing the tech "from proof-of-concept to a validated prototype operational system."
records.notes: Directly fetched; corroborates and adds detail to the NETL program page (L04-026). | Directly fetched by scout; corroborates and adds detail to L04-026 (NETL page, ultimately rejected as inaccessible) and is itself corroborated by L04-039 (SwRI). Retained as-is.

records.title: STEP Demo pilot plant achieves full operational conditions for Phase 1 of testing
records.authors_or_org: Southwest Research Institute (SwRI)
records.claim_supported: On 2024-10-16, the $169M STEP Demo sCO2 pilot plant (GTI Energy, SwRI, GE Vernova Advanced Research, DOE/NETL) reached full operating conditions: ~4 MW net power synchronized to the grid, 500C turbine inlet temperature, 27,000 rpm.
records.notes: Directly fetched; SwRI is the operating research institute for the demonstration, making this a primary project-status record rather than pure trade press -- tiered T1. | Directly fetched by scout; SwRI is the operating research institute for the demonstration, making this a primary project-status record rather than pure trade press -- tiered T1. Note: a later independent milestone/ribbon-cutting for the same facility (~$155M cost figure, ~10MW / table-sized framing) is separately corroborated in Chinese-language Taiwan media, see L04-054.

records.title: NET Power's First Allam Cycle 300-MW Gas-Fired Project Will Be Built in Texas
records.authors_or_org: POWER Magazine
records.claim_supported: NET Power's first commercial 300 MW Allam-cycle plant (Project Permian/"Serial Number One") is being built at an Occidental-hosted site near Odessa, TX; captures ~860,000 t CO2/yr; construction started Q3 2024, targeted online 2026-2028; industry capex estimates for similar Allam-cycle plants run $900-1,200/kW; financing to combine NET Power capital, shareholder investment, project financing, and potential 45Q tax credits.
records.notes: Directly fetched. | Directly fetched by scout. Retained as-is.

records.title: Rondo deploys Southeast Asia's 'first industrial Heat Battery'
records.authors_or_org: Energy-Storage.News
records.claim_supported: Rondo Energy deployed a 33 MWh Rondo Heat Battery at SCG Cleanergy's cement plant in Saraburi, Thailand -- described as the first commercial heat-battery system at a cement facility, integrated with existing heat-recovery infrastructure to drive a steam turbine, reported 2025-11-17.
records.notes: Thailand is not one of the six core Asian markets (CN/JP/KR/TW/IN/SG) named in the mission's Asia-geography assumption, so this record is not counted toward that specific 8-source quota, but it is retained as directly relevant Southeast Asian demand evidence for the heat-to-power lane. | Directly fetched by scout. Thailand is not one of the six core Asian markets (CN/JP/KR/TW/IN/SG) named in the mission's Asia-geography assumption, so this record is not counted toward that specific quota, but it is retained as directly relevant Southeast Asian demand evidence for the heat-to-power lane.

records.title: Rondo Powers Up World's Largest Industrial Heat Battery
records.authors_or_org: Rondo Energy
records.claim_supported: Rondo Energy's 100 MWh Heat Battery (RHB) began daily automatic operation around 2025-10-16, powered exclusively by 20 MW of on-site solar PV, delivering continuous high-pressure industrial steam to a fuel-production facility in California -- described as the world's largest industrial heat battery. Storage medium is brick and wire at >1000C with a claimed round-trip efficiency above 97%.
records.notes: SWAPPED canonical URL from the scout's original canarymedia.com pick (which returned 403 on every attempt this session, both to the verifier and originally to the scout) to Rondo's own primary press release, which was successfully fetched and covers the identical facts. Tier upgraded to T1 to match the convention applied elsewhere in this ledger (L04-045/046/049/050) for a vendor's own primary announcement of a named deployment. The customer facility is described only as "a fuel production facility in California" by Rondo itself; a specific customer name ("Holmes Western Oil Corporation") appeared only in third-party WebSearch snippets this session and was NOT independently confirmed by direct fetch, so it is omitted from claim_supported pending confirmation.
